title: 10 Proven Herbal Tinctures for Treating Premenstrual Syndrome Symptoms

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, which are common symptoms of PMS.
Agnuside is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has a calming effect on the body, reducing symptoms of anxiety, irritability, and mood swings associated with PMS.
Iridoids help with premenstrual syndrome because they have a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and emotional changes that often occur before a woman's period starts.

Saponins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory, which in turn helps to ease symptoms such as mood swings and bloating.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitex agnus-castus tincture is a herbal remedy that has been used for centuries to help ease symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). It's a plant-based extract that can help balance the body's hormonal levels, which is often a cause of PMS symptoms. Women who take Vitex agnus-castus tincture may notice a reduction in mood swings, anxiety, and irritability that often come with PMS. It can also help regulate menstrual cycles, which can lead to fewer cramps, bloating, and breast tenderness. Additionally, Vitex agnus-castus tincture may help reduce hot flashes and night sweats, which can be a symptom of PMS in some women. Many women find that taking Vitex agnus-castus tincture during the premenstrual phase of their cycle helps to alleviate these symptoms, allowing them to feel more balanced and in control.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tinctures that help with premenstrual syndrome, you will need dried Valerian root and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. First, start by measuring one part of dried Valerian root to two parts of vodka or glycerin in a clean glass jar. For example, if you are using 100 grams of Valerian root, you will need 200 grams of vodka or glycerin. Next, close the jar and shake it well to combine the ingredients. Store the jar in a cool, dark place for two to three weeks, shaking the jar every day or two to help the Valerian root release its active ingredients into the solvent. After the two to three weeks, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. The resulting liquid is your Valeriana officinalis tincture, which can be taken as needed to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as anxiety, mood swings, and insomnia.
remedies.image_alt: Vitex agnus-castus herbal tinctures for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Phenolic acids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory agent, which eases symptoms such as mood swings and cramps.
Isoferulic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps reduce stress and anxiety by acting as a natural sedative, thereby easing symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.
Actein is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has a sedative effect that helps reduce anxiety and stress, and its ability to relax the muscles can ease symptoms such as cramps and bloating.

Ferulic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce stress and anxiety by acting as a natural sedative, thereby providing relief from symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.
Lignans are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have a relaxing effect on the body, reducing anxiety, insomnia, and other symptoms caused by hormonal changes during the menstrual cycle.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemona tincture is an herbal remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This is because it contains a compound called actein, which has been shown to ease symptoms like mood swings, bloating, and cramps. The tincture works by balancing the body's hormonal levels, particularly estrogen, which can become imbalanced before menstruation. This balance helps to reduce symptoms associated with PMS, such as anxiety and irritability. Additionally, the tincture has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to ease physical discomfort like cramps and breast tenderness. Some women also find that it helps to regulate their menstrual cycle, making it a popular natural remedy for PMS. When taken as directed, Cimicifuga racemona tincture can be a safe and effective way to manage PMS symptoms.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis tinctures that help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS), you will need dried valerian root, a solvent like vodka or glycerin, and a clean glass jar. First, start by measuring out one part of dried valerian root to two parts of solvent in the jar. For example, if you have one ounce of dried valerian root, use two ounces of vodka. Next, seal the jar tightly and let it sit in a cool, dark place for two to three weeks. This will allow the valerian root to infuse into the solvent and create the tincture. After the infusion time has passed, strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. Your valerian tincture is now ready to use. Take 20-30 drops of the tincture, two to three times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of PMS, such as anxiety, cramps, and mood swings.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Paeoniflorin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has been found to reduce anxiety and insomnia by acting as a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory, which helps to calm the body and mind during the menstrual cycle.

Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory agent, which in turn can help alleviate symptoms such as mood swings and breast tenderness.

Polyphenols help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms by reducing anxiety and inflammation, promoting relaxation, and regulating hormonal fluctuations, which eases menstrual cramp discomfort and emotional mood swings.

Tannins, also known as valerian root, are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural sedative and antioxidant, which can help to calm the mind and body and alleviate symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.
Quercetin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce inflammation and relax muscles, which can ease symptoms such as bloating, mood swings, and cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Paeonia lactiflora tincture is often used to help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This is because it's believed to have a calming effect on the body and mind. The tincture is made from the roots of the peony plant, which contains a compound called paeoniflorin. This compound is thought to help reduce anxiety and stress, which are common symptoms of PMS. It may also help to regulate hormones and ease physical symptoms like bloating and cramps. Some people find that taking Paeonia lactiflora tincture helps to stabilize their mood and reduce irritability, which can be helpful during the premenstrual phase. Additionally, the tincture may help to improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering dried Valerian root, a glass jar with a lid, and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. Begin by placing 2-3 tablespoons of the dried root into the glass jar, then fill the jar with the solvent, making sure that the root is completely covered. Seal the jar tightly and let it sit in a cool, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every day or two. This process is called maceration, where the root's active compounds dissolve into the solvent. After the maceration period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another container, discarding the solids. Finally, transfer the tincture into dark glass bottles and store them in a cool, dark place. To use the tincture, take 20-30 drops of it 2-3 times a day, mixed with water or another liquid. Valeriana officinalis is known for its calming and soothing effects, which can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety, mood swings, and restlessness.

Phenolic acids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, which are common symptoms of PMS.

Ligustilide is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has been shown to help reduce anxiety and stress levels, relieve menstrual cramps, and improve mood in women experiencing premenstrual syndrome symptoms.

Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to calm the nervous system, reduce anxiety, and ease physical symptoms such as cramps and mood swings, making it a natural remedy for women experiencing PMS.

Butylphthalide is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has been shown to help reduce anxiety, relieve stress, and promote relaxation by acting on the brain's neurotransmitters and hormones, thus easing the symptoms of PMS such as mood swings, irritability, and emotional upset.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica tincture is a natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The root of the angelica plant has been used for centuries in traditional medicine to ease menstrual cramps, bloating, and mood swings. The tincture contains compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and pain in the uterus. It also has antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the muscles and reduce menstrual cramps. Additionally, angelica archangelica has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help reduce anxiety and irritability often experienced during PMS. Some women find that taking angelica archangelica tincture helps regulate their menstrual cycle and reduce symptoms associated with PMS. It's essential to note that individual results may vary, and it's recommended to consult with a healthcare professional before using any new herbal remedy.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tinctures that help with premenstrual syndrome, you'll need to gather the necessary materials. Start by sourcing high-quality dried Valerian root, which is the active ingredient responsible for its calming effects. Next, combine 1 part of the dried root with 2-3 parts of a neutral solvent like vodka or glycerin in a clean glass jar. For example, you can use 1 ounce of dried root with 2-3 ounces of vodka. Secure the lid and store the jar in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking the jar occasionally to help the solvent extract the root's active compounds. After the extraction period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. The resulting tincture can be taken in small doses, usually 20-30 drops, 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety, mood swings, and cramps. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedy, especially if you're pregnant or breastfeeding.

Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to calm the nervous system, reduce anxiety and stress, and promote relaxation, which can alleviate symptoms of PMS such as mood swings, irritability, and depression.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hypericum perforatum, also known as St. John's Wort, is a plant that has been used for centuries to help with mood swings and other symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The tincture made from this plant is believed to help reduce stress and anxiety that can lead to irritability and mood swings during the premenstrual phase. It is thought to have a balancing effect on the body's hormones, which can help ease symptoms like bloating, cramps, and breast tenderness. The tincture is also believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce physical symptoms like headaches and fatigue. Some people find that taking Hypericum perforatum tincture helps them feel more calm and relaxed, which can be especially helpful during the premenstrual phase when emotions can be intense. Additionally, this tincture is often used in combination with other herbal remedies to create a customized treatment plan for managing PMS symptoms.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture, also known as valerian root tincture, which can help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by gathering high-quality valerian root and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. Clean and dry the valerian roots, then chop them into small pieces to increase their surface area. Combine the chopped roots with the chosen solvent in a clean glass jar, making sure the roots are completely covered. The general ratio is one part valerian root to two parts solvent, but this can vary depending on personal preference. Seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place, allowing the mixture to steep for at least two weeks, shaking the jar daily. This process is called maceration, allowing the active compounds from the valerian root to infuse into the solvent. After the steeping period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding the solids. The resulting tincture can be stored in a cool, dark place and taken as needed, usually in small amounts of 20-30 drops, diluted in water or taken sublingually.

Zingerone is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, and also acts as a natural pain reliever to alleviate symptoms such as headaches and cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, is a plant that has been used for centuries to help with many health issues. For women experiencing premenstrual syndrome (PMS), ginger tincture can be a helpful remedy. PMS is a condition where women feel tired, bloated, and irritable before their period starts. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and ease cramps, making it a good choice for relieving PMS symptoms. Ginger also has a natural warming effect, which can help relax the muscles and improve blood flow. Additionally, ginger has been shown to have a positive effect on nausea and vomiting, which are common symptoms of PMS. By taking ginger tincture, women may find that their symptoms are reduced and they are able to feel more comfortable during this time.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture that can help with premenstrual syndrome, you will need dried valerian root and a high-proof alcohol like vodka or rum. First, start by making a strong tea by steeping one tablespoon of dried valerian root in one cup of boiling water for 5-10 minutes, then let it cool. Next, combine the cooled tea with a cup of high-proof alcohol in a clean glass jar. Make sure the valerian root tea covers the alcohol, and seal the jar tightly. Store the jar in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking the jar every few days. This will allow the valerian root to infuse its medicinal properties into the alcohol. After the infusion period, strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids. The resulting valerian tincture can be taken in small amounts, typically 20-30 drops, 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety, insomnia, and mood swings.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal tinctures for premenstrual syndrome

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica sinensis tincture is a natural remedy that may help relieve symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). It contains a group of compounds called coumarins, which are known to have anti-inflammatory and anti-anxiety effects. These compounds can help reduce bloating, breast tenderness, and mood swings often experienced during PMS. Additionally, Angelica sinensis tincture may also help alleviate fatigue and anxiety, which can be common symptoms of PMS. Some studies have shown that Angelica sinensis extracts have a positive effect on mood, reducing symptoms of depression and anxiety. As a result, many women turn to Angelica sinensis tincture as a natural way to manage their PMS symptoms and improve their overall well-being.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture that helps with premenstrual syndrome, you will need dried valerian roots and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. Start by gathering a handful of dried valerian roots and placing them in a clean glass jar. Next, pour enough solvent over the roots to cover them completely, making sure not to overfill the jar. Seal the jar tightly and place it in a cool, dark place, such as a cupboard or pantry, for 4-6 weeks. During this time, the solvent will extract the medicinal properties from the valerian roots, creating a strong tincture. After 4-6 weeks, strain the tincture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids. Store the tincture in a cool, dark place and use it according to the recommended dosage, usually 20-30 drops taken 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as mood swings, anxiety, and cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba tincture is sometimes used to help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. This is because Ginkgo biloba contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which are chemicals that may help to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the body. PMS symptoms like breast tenderness and mood swings can be caused by hormonal changes that affect blood flow and cause inflammation in the body. By improving blood flow and reducing inflammation, Ginkgo biloba tincture may help to alleviate these symptoms. Additionally, Ginkgo biloba has been shown to have a positive effect on neurotransmitters in the brain, which can help to reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression that often accompany PMS. Some women also report that Ginkgo biloba helps to improve their sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS. However, it's essential to talk to a healthcare provider before using Ginkgo biloba tincture for PMS symptoms, as it may interact with certain medications or have side effects.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture for premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering dried valerian root, a clean glass jar with a lid, vodka or glycerin as a solvent, and a strainer. Begin by measuring one part of dried valerian root to two parts of vodka or glycerin in the glass jar. For example, use 100 grams of valerian root and 200 grams of vodka. Close the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking the jar every day. This process is called maceration, which allows the valerian root's active compounds to infuse into the solvent. After the maceration period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean container, pressing on the solids to extract as much liquid as possible. Discard the solids and store the valerian tincture in a cool, dark place. Use 20-30 drops of the tincture, 2-3 times a day, mixed with water or taken sublingually, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as anxiety, mood swings, and insomnia.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia, also known as English lavender, has been used for centuries to help with various health issues, including premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The tincture made from its flowers contains chemicals that have a soothing effect on the body. It helps to calm the mind and reduce feelings of anxiety and stress, which are common symptoms of PMS. The antispasmodic properties in the tincture also help to ease cramps and discomfort in the lower abdomen, making it a natural remedy for menstrual cramps. Additionally, the tincture's ability to regulate hormones and balance the body's chemical levels can help to stabilize mood swings and emotional changes that occur during PMS. This makes Lavandula angustifolia tincture a potential natural aid for women experiencing PMS symptoms.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tincture to help with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering the herb, also known as valerian root. You'll need about one pound of dried root, which can be bought from a health food store or grown yourself. Next, fill a clean glass jar with the dried valerian root, leaving about an inch of space at the top. Pour a neutral carrier oil, such as grapeseed oil, over the herb until the jar is full. The general ratio is one part herb to two parts oil. Stir the mixture well and seal the jar. Let it sit in a cool, dark place for two to four weeks, shaking the jar every few days. This process is called maceration, and it helps to release the herb's active properties into the oil. After the maceration period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle. Discard the solids and store the tincture in the fridge. To use the tincture, take one to two teaspoons as needed, either alone or mixed with water or another liquid. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using valerian root for premenstrual syndrome, especially if you're pregnant or breastfeeding.

Isovaleric acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural GABA (inhibitory neurotransmitter) receptor agonist, thereby promoting relaxation and alleviating symptoms of PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian, is a natural herb that has been used for centuries to help with sleep and relaxation. For women who experience premenstrual syndrome (PMS), valerian tincture can be a helpful remedy. PMS symptoms like anxiety, mood swings, and cramps can be reduced with valerian's calming effects. The herb contains compounds like valerenic acid and isovaleric acid, which help to calm the nervous system and promote relaxation. When taken as a tincture, valerian can help to reduce PMS symptoms by promoting better sleep, reducing anxiety, and relieving cramps. Additionally, valerian has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce bloating and breast tenderness that often accompany PMS. Many women find that taking valerian tincture a few days before their period starts helps to alleviate their symptoms and improve their overall well-being.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Valeriana officinalis herbal tinctures that help with premenstrual syndrome, you will need dried Valerian root, a clean glass jar with a lid, a carrier oil like vodka or glycerin, and a dark glass bottle with a dropper lid. First, prepare the dried Valerian root by crushing it into small pieces using a mortar and pestle or a coffee grinder. Next, place the crushed root into the clean glass jar, leaving about an inch of space at the top. Pour the carrier oil over the root, making sure that the root is completely covered. Then, seal the jar with the lid and let it sit in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking the jar every day to help the root and oil blend together. After the steeping time, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into the dark glass bottle, discarding the solids. Store the tincture in the dark glass bottle with the dropper lid, and use 20-30 drops of the tincture in water or tea as needed to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal tinctures for premenstrual syndrome

intro_desc: Many women experience premenstrual syndrome (PMS), which can cause physical and emotional symptoms like bloating, mood swings, and fatigue. Herbal tinctures can help alleviate these symptoms. Vitex agnus-castus, also known as chaste tree, is a popular herb for PMS. It helps regulate hormonal balance, particularly estrogen and progesterone, which can become imbalanced leading to PMS symptoms. This herb also helps reduce anxiety and stress, making it easier to cope with mood swings.  Cimicifuga racemosa, or black cohosh, is another herb that can aid in reducing PMS symptoms. It has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate physical symptoms like bloating and cramps. Black cohosh also has a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and stress. By using herbal tinctures like Vitex agnus-castus and Cimicifuga racemosa, women can experience relief from PMS symptoms, improving their overall well-being.  Fixing PMS issues is crucial because these symptoms can significantly impact daily life. Women with PMS may avoid social events, struggle with relationships, and have difficulty concentrating at work due to fatigue and mood swings. By managing PMS symptoms, women can regain control over their lives, improve their relationships, and enhance their productivity.
supplementary_best: A combination of herbal tinctures may help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms. Vitex agnus-castus, also known as chaste tree, can regulate menstrual cycles and reduce PMS symptoms such as mood swings and breast tenderness. Cimicifuga racemosa, or black cohosh, has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help ease cramps and spasms.  Paeonia lactiflora, or peony, has a calming effect and can reduce anxiety and irritability often associated with PMS. Angelica sinensis, or dong quai, is a traditional Chinese herb that can ease menstrual cramps and promote relaxation. Zingiber officinale, or ginger, has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and discomfort.  A combination of these herbs may be the most effective in addressing PMS symptoms due to their complementary properties. Vitex agnus-castus and Cimicifuga racemosa can regulate and ease menstrual symptoms, while Paeonia lactiflora, Angelica sinensis, and Zingiber officinale can promote relaxation and reduce pain.
supplementary_avoid: If you have premenstrual syndrome (PMS), you should avoid certain herbal tinctures that can worsen symptoms or interact with medications. One such herb is 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' or licorice root, which can increase blood pressure and worsen water retention, exacerbating PMS symptoms like bloating and mood swings. 'Dong quai' or 'Angelica keiskeana' should also be avoided as it may cause or worsen bleeding problems. 'Scutellaria baicalensis' or Chinese skullcap can be toxic to the liver and kidneys, especially when combined with medications. 'Ephedra sinica' or Ma Huang can raise blood pressure and worsen anxiety and insomnia associated with PMS. 'Aconitum spp.' or Monk's hood contains a toxic alkaloid that can cause heart problems, nausea, and dizziness, which can be severe in PMS sufferers.
supplementary_other_remedies: One natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) is dietary changes. Eating foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids, such as salmon and walnuts, can help reduce inflammation and improve mood. Omega-3 fatty acids have anti-inflammatory properties that can ease symptoms like bloating and breast tenderness. Additionally, foods high in complex carbohydrates, like whole grains and fruits, can help stabilize blood sugar levels and reduce symptoms like anxiety and irritability. Magnesium-rich foods like dark leafy greens, nuts, and seeds can also help alleviate symptoms like muscle cramps and fatigue.  Other natural remedies that can help with PMS include warm baths and aromatherapy. Soaking in a warm bath with Epsom salts can help relax the body and reduce symptoms like cramps and bloating. Aromatherapy with essential oils like lavender and chamomile can also help calm the mind and reduce anxiety and stress. These remedies can help regulate the body's hormonal balance and reduce symptoms associated with PMS.  Another natural remedy is getting regular exercise. Engaging in physical activity, such as yoga or walking, can help reduce symptoms like anxiety and depression. Exercise can also help improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS. Furthermore, regular exercise can help reduce stress and improve mood, making it easier to manage PMS symptoms.  Some women also find relief with acupressure. Applying pressure to specific points on the body, such as the LI4 point on the web between the thumb and index finger, can help alleviate symptoms like cramps and bloating. This ancient practice can help balance the body's energy and reduce symptoms associated with PMS.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) often experience other health issues due to hormonal imbalances and nutritional deficiencies. One common complaint is fatigue, which can be alleviated with ashwagandha, a herb that reduces stress and anxiety. The root of the ashwagandha plant contains compounds that help regulate cortisol levels, leading to improved energy and better sleep.  Another issue faced by individuals with PMS is anxiety and depression, which can be managed with St. John's Wort. This herb contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that have a calming effect on the nervous system, helping to reduce symptoms of depression and anxiety.  Women with PMS also often experience digestive problems, such as bloating and cramps, which can be treated with peppermint and ginger. Peppermint oil has anti-inflammatory properties that ease cramps and digestive discomfort, while ginger has anti-inflammatory and anti-nausea properties that alleviate bloating and other symptoms.  Additionally, PMS sufferers may experience mood swings and irritability, which can be reduced with passionflower. This herb has flavonoids and alkaloids that help regulate neurotransmitters, leading to improved mood and reduced anxiety.  Many women with PMS also experience headaches and migraines, which can be managed with feverfew. This herb contains compounds that prevent blood vessels from dilating, reducing the severity of headaches and migraines.
